entities.name: WHEAT
entities.description: The primary cereal crop studied for yield and protein responses

entities.name: YIELD GAP
entities.description: The difference between potential and actual crop yield

entities.name: NITROGEN
entities.description: A primary nutrient driver of the wheat yield gap

entities.name: NITROGEN USE EFFICIENCY
entities.description: The efficiency with which wheat utilizes nitrogen fertilizer

entities.name: PHOSPHORUS
entities.description: A nutrient co-applied with nitrogen to potentially increase NUE

entities.name: LOW RAINFALL
entities.description: Environmental condition characterized by limited precipitation

entities.name: SOIL
entities.description: The growth medium, varying across dune, mid-slope, and swale

entities.name: SOUTH AUSTRALIA
entities.description: The geographic region where the field trials were conducted

entities.name: PROTEIN
entities.description: The protein content of the wheat grain

entities.name: SANDY SOILS
entities.description: Soil types including dune and mid-slope that showed linear yield responses

entities.name: CLAY LOAM
entities.description: Soil type (swale) that showed yield response only in high rainfall seasons

entities.name: N FERTILISER APPLICATION
entities.description: The application of nitrogen at levels of 0, 10, 20, 40, and 80 kg N ha-1

entities.name: P FERTILISER APPLICATION
entities.description: The application of phosphorus at levels of 0, 5, 10, and 20 kg P ha-1

entities.name: SEMI-ARID ENVIRONMENT
entities.description: The climatic setting of the study area in South Australia

relationships.description: Supply of nitrogen is a primary driver of the yield gap
Nitrogen is the nutrient whose use efficiency is being measured
Co-application of phosphorus is suggested to increase nitrogen use efficiency
Nitrogen fertilizer input affects the yield of wheat
Phosphorus nutrition was measured for its effect on increasing NUE
Yield is a measured trait of wheat
Protein is a measured trait of wheat
Wheat was grown in South Australia
Wheat was grown in a semi-arid environment
Sandy soils produced significant and linear yield responses
Clay loam produced yield response only in high rainfall seasons
The study aims to close the yield gap for wheat
Wheat is grown as a low rainfall cereal crop in this context
